content:
First, recall that every even perfect number has the form  
  \(2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\)  
where \(2^p-1\) is a Mersenne prime.  There are no known odd perfect numbers, so if \(n^n+1\) is perfect it must be even of the above form.

1.  If \(n\) were even then \(n^n\) is even and \(n^n+1\) is odd.  No odd perfect numbers are known, so we may discard even \(n\).  
2.  Hence \(n\) is odd, so \(n^n\) is odd and \(n^n+1\) is even.  We set  
       n^n+1 \;=\; 2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\,.  
3.  Now check small odd \(n\):  
   – \(n=1:\quad1^1+1=2\) (not perfect).  
   – \(n=3:\quad3^3+1=27+1=28=2^2\cdot7\), and indeed \(7=2^3-1\) is prime, so \(28\) is perfect.  
   – For \(n\ge5\), one quickly sees \(n^n+1\) does not match any of the known even perfect numbers 
     \(6,28,496,8128,33550336,\dots\).  

No other solutions occur.  Therefore the only positive integer \(n\) with \(n^n+1\) perfect is

\boxed{3}.

3.  Now check small odd \(n\):  
   – \(n=1:\quad1^1+1=2\) (not perfect).

   – For \(n\ge5\), one quickly sees \(n^n+1\) does not match any of the known even perfect numbers

\boxed{3}.
